title: South Korea makes AI access a universal right, plans free chatbot for 51 million
subtitle: The AI Basic Act amendment took effect July 21 and MSIT is standing up 'AI for Everyone' — a domestic-model-backed chatbot free to every resident through 2028.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: A partial amendment to South Korea's AI Basic Act took legal effect on July 21, and Minister of Science and ICT Bae Kyung-hoon announced the same day that the government will give all 51 million residents free, unlimited access to an AI chatbot by year-end. Two or three private companies will be selected to build and operate the service, which must run on domestic Korean AI models for at least 50 percent of its stack. A beta chatbot is planned for late September, with a full service — including an AI agent that proactively identifies government benefits users are eligible for — targeted before December. The service is funded to remain free through 2028.

The mechanism to watch is the domestic-model requirement. About 44.5 percent of Koreans — roughly 23 million people — already use generative AI regularly, and ChatGPT holds the largest share of that user base. Only about 1.8 million pay. Seoul is betting that a free, government-funded chatbot backed by Korean models can shift a mass user base off foreign services while giving domestic labs a guaranteed distribution channel large enough to justify continued frontier investment. It is industrial policy dressed as universal access.

This is the first G20 government to treat frontier AI access the way earlier generations treated electricity or broadband — as a service the state guarantees. It follows Korea's earlier framework for national AI sovereignty and lands as the EU AI Act enters its enforcement window and the White House finalizes a voluntary frontier review framework. Three different regulatory postures — mandatory rules in Europe, voluntary review in the United States, state-provided access in Korea — are running as parallel experiments in the same twelve months.

For learners: 'AI access is a public right' is a policy claim, not a technical one. What Korea is really deciding is who owns the users' data, whose model gets to be the default, and how public benefits flow through an AI agent. If you are studying policy, that is where the interesting questions live. If you are studying engineering, this is a preview of what state-scale AI deployments will demand — uptime, auditability, and interfaces good enough that a 78-year-old pensioner can file a benefit claim without a call center.

sources.title: South Korea promises free homegrown AI chatbot this year — TechXplore/AFP
